title: Modi's Rome Reset: India Clears the Debris in Italy Ties
lede: At the G20 Summit, India's Prime Minister and Italy's new technocrat leader agree to move past two poisoned cases that had frozen bilateral relations for a decade.

sections.context:
India's relationship with Italy had become a frozen asset. Two cases—the AgustaWestland helicopter procurement scandal and the Enrica Lexie marine incident—had calcified into symbols of bilateral dysfunction, blocking deeper engagement even as both nations shared Mediterranean and European interests. The arrival of Mario Draghi as Italian Prime Minister in February 2021 offered an opening that the Modi government was strategically positioned to exploit. Rome, hosting the G20 presidency, provided the institutional theater for recalibration. Modi's bilateral with Draghi signals that New Delhi has decided the cost of maintaining grievance over these legacy disputes outweighs the strategic benefit of their continued friction. The timing matters: Italy holds the G20 chair during a period of fractured global governance, and India, as an emerging pole in the multipolar world order, cannot afford to leave potentially aligned European partners in a state of mutual suspicion.

The bilateral meeting was more than ceremonial window-dressing. Both sides committed explicitly to "revitalise" ties—a term that presupposes acknowledgment that current relations were withered. This is diplomatic language for accepting that neither side will achieve courtroom victories on the historical cases, and that continued litigation of these matters serves neither nation's interests. Draghi, a former European Central Bank president, represents a European leadership class focused on pragmatic statecraft over ideological grievance. Modi, similarly, has shown willingness to compartmentalize disputes when strategic alignment beckons. The G20 summit provided cover for both sides to move forward without appearing to capitulate: they were engaged in the business of global economic coordination, not settling scores.
sections.threads:
This reset reflects a broader [[india-italy-marine-firing-2012|pattern of India resolving bilateral friction with European partners]]. The Enrica Lexie case—involving Italian marines accused of killing Indian fishermen in 2012—had created deep public and institutional resentment in India. Yet it mirrors India's pragmatic handling of comparable maritime incidents with other partners: the management of tensions has ultimately yielded to partnership imperatives. Similarly, the AgustaWestland scandal, involving allegations of corruption in a defense procurement, had poisoned both the bilateral relationship and India's defense procurement credibility more broadly. By agreeing to move past it, Modi and Draghi are following the [[india-european-defense-cooperation-expansion|larger European trajectory of India's strategic partnerships]].

The bilateral also sits within a wider European recalibration toward India. Italy, as a G7 member and EU player, had been semi-peripheral to India's European strategy, which traditionally emphasized France and Germany as primary continental anchors. The reset with Draghi signals that New Delhi is systematizing its European engagement, recognizing that a fragmented approach to EU members leaves India vulnerable to coordinated European positions that do not reflect India's interests. France's role as a strategic partner is not diminished, but Italy is being elevated from transactional to strategic status—a shift that requires clearing historical debris first.

Draghi's own positioning within Europe matters. As an unelected technocrat leading a coalition government, Draghi lacks the domestic political mandate to sustain grudges against foreign powers. This creates an alignment with Modi: both leaders operate in systems where executive action can move faster than legislative or public sentiment would otherwise permit. Modi's ability to broker agreements without needing parliamentary ratification for every dimension of bilateral reset gave him flexibility that previous Indian governments lacked.
sections.signal:
Watch for three specific indicators of whether this Rome reset translates into substantive partnership. First, monitor [[india-italy-defense-cooperation-2022-2023|defense cooperation announcements in the 12 months following the G20 summit]]. If the commitment to revitalize ties is genuine, India should expect formal defense procurement dialogues, joint exercises, or expanded military-to-military contact. The absence of these concrete mechanisms within 18 months would signal that Rome's pivot was rhetorical rather than strategic.

Second, track whether Italy moves toward greater alignment with India's positions on UN Security Council matters and broader global governance questions. Italy's voting patterns on resolutions pertaining to Kashmir, nuclear non-proliferation, or development issues will reveal whether the bilateral reset extends to geopolitical coordination or remains confined to clearing historical irritants. A meaningful partnership would produce observable alignment on 2-3 significant international votes within the next two years.

Third, observe whether the AgustaWestland and Enrica Lexie cases see formal closure mechanisms—whether through courts, commissions, or diplomatic archives—before the end of 2023. If these cases continue to generate legal or media friction, it signals that the Rome agreement was damage control rather than genuine reset. Conversely, if both sides manage parallel closure of these disputes without either side claiming victory, the bilateral will have achieved the threshold of genuine [[india-italy-relationship-trajectory-2024|partnership normalization that allows for future strategic expansion]].
